highlights:
Director- Technical Product, POS | Hirebase

Back to Job Board

# Director- Technical Product, POS

Remote, USA

Posted 2026-05-26

Full-time

We are looking for an experienced and innovative Director- Technical Product, POS to join Yum! Brands. This position requires a strong technical background, a passion for driving business results, and the ability to lead a team. As the Director- Technical Product, POS, you will lead the design, development, and implementation of our point-of-sale (POS) system. The ideal candidate for this role should have at least five years of experience in software development and have a proven track record in leading technical product teams. The ideal candidate must also have a deep understanding of POS systems and be able to manage complex projects and timelines. Additionally, the Director- Technical Product, POS will need to be highly organized, able to communicate effectively with stakeholders, and have excellent problem-solving skills. If you have the qualifications and experience necessary to be successful in this role, we’d love to hear from you.

Yum! Brands is an Equal Opportunity Employer. We celebrate diversity and are committed to creating an inclusive environment for all employees. We do not discriminate based upon race, religion, color, national origin, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, age, status as a protected veteran, status as an individual with a disability, or other applicable legally protected characteristics.

results.title: Principal Product Manager – Mobile at Yum! Center for Global Franchise Excellence - United States Remote
highlights: • Define and continuously evolve a multi‑year product vision and strategy for the mobile app aligned to company goals and customer needs. • Set clear, outcome‑based objectives (OKRs) and ensure priorities reflect the highest customer and business value. • Translate strategy into a focused portfolio of initiatives that balance offense (growth) and defense (stability, reliability, technical debt). • Lead an empowered product squad—especially with engineering and design to identify opportunities, explore solutions, and deliver measurable outcomes (not just features). • Establish strong product principles and decision frameworks so teams can move fast with clarity and integrity. • Drive digital innovation through emerging technologies and continuously evaluate and influence KFC's position to be able to deliver differentiated customer experiences. • Create an environment where teams tackle risks early (value/usability/feasibility/viability) through discovery, prototyping, and experimentation. • Drive continuous discovery and delivery using customer research, analytics, and experiments to validate ideas before scaling solutions. • Develop a deep understanding of customers and operators using qualitative and quantitative insights to uncover unmet needs and friction points. • Champion field immersion, partnering with market and operations leaders to validate solutions in real restaurant environments and ensure they scale under operational complexity and peak‑demand conditions. • Drive data‑informed decision‑making by defining success metrics, evaluating product performance, and steering rapid iteration to deliver measurable business and guest outcomes. • Forecast the approximate benefit of initiatives using benchmarks and prior learnings; define measurement plans upfront so key metrics are treated as P0 requirements (instrumentation is not an afterthought). • Ruthlessly simplify: repeatedly capture ~80% of value with ~20% of effort, pushing teams toward the smallest solution that achieves the outcome without sacrificing quality or integrity. • Sequence work to optimize compounding impact, balancing quick wins vs platform investments and ensuring the roadmap remains outcome based. • Grind it out to ship: remove obstacles, align stakeholders, and drive end to end execution through launch, adoption, and iteration. • Collaborate across Marketing, Operations, Technology, Finance, Legal, and Support to integrate product plans and deliver a seamless end to end guest experience. • Build credibility and trust across a matrixed organization; earn the right to make decisions by being prepared, data informed, and relentlessly focused on business outcomes.

results.title: Yum! Brands Leading the Way in Digital Restaurant Technology - Food & Beverage Outlook
highlights:
Setting the bar for modern restaurant technology and digital experiences, Yum! Brands is upskilling its technical teams via a ground-breaking learning program – Tech Elevate.
[...]
The stalwart company is adamant that the future of the QSR sector will be determined by technology and is working toward this goal by upskilling its digital and technology teams through a flagship learning program called Tech Elevate.
[...]
“Tech Elevate reinforces Yum! Digital & Technology’s commitment to continuous learning and technical excellence. What sets Tech Elevate apart is that it serves as a functional development program for software engineers and employees in adjacent roles, helping them strengthen technical skills and foster greater collaboration across Byte by Yum! Teams and our brands,” states Shiv Adhiappan, Pizza Hut Chief Technology & Information Officer and Tech Elevate Executive Sponsor.
[...]
Approximately 300 employees have signed up to take part in the second iteration of the program. This online, six-to-nine-week initiative will provide the participants with the skills and tools needed to deepen their technical abilities across a range of critical technologies. Training topics include AI and machine learning, various programming languages, modern web development, and site reliability engineering.
[...]
New this year are AI platform engineering, Byte Menu, and application programming interface query languages.
[...]
Through Tech Elevate, Yum! Brands team members are provided with intentional time and space to build skills that directly support platform scalability, reliability, and innovation at its finest.
[...]
This second version of the Tech Elevate program allows participants to choose from three courses, including ‘AI-First Platform & Reliability Engineering,’ ‘Foundations of Byte Menu,’ and the final course offering highlights the execution, federation, and best practices of API query languages.
[...]
Saurabh Sundriyal, senior software engineer II and engineering team lead at KFC US, notes that “The AI-First Platform & Reliability Engineering course was extremely practical and immediately applicable to my day-to-day work. I used the learnings right away to enhance application logging and built dashboards using large language models, improving visibility and operational efficiency for my team at KFC US.”
[...]
As a steadfast leader in the QSR industry, Yum! Brands is continuing to invest in its people to ensure the organization helps shape the way in the constant technological evolution of the sector. Tech Elevate allows individuals the opportunity to build and sharpen the critical skills needed to develop technology that creates better teams and customer experiences.
[...]
“As our technology stack evolves and scales globally, our teams must grow alongside it. This long-term investment in our people will ensure that we have the skills, confidence, and discipline to continue to grow our brands,” concludes Adhiappan.

results.title: Tech Elevate: Yum! Brands Fuels Digital Transformation with Workforce Upskilling | Edubild AI Insights | EdubildAI
highlights:
In an era where digital transformation is the cornerstone of competitive advantage, Yum! Brands is making significant strides by investing in its workforce. The company, known for its vast portfolio of fast-food restaurants, has launched Tech Elevate, a program designed to enhance the digital skills of its employees. This initiative is not just about keeping up with the times; it's about setting the pace for digital innovation in the Quick Service Restaurant (QSR) industry.
[...]
Tech Elevate is a six-to-nine-week program aimed at equipping nearly 300 employees with cutting-edge skills in digital technology. The program is part of Yum!'s broader strategy to lead in digital restaurant technology by enhancing the capabilities of its Digital and Technology teams. Yum! Brands, headquartered in Louisville, Kentucky, is leveraging this initiative to ensure its workforce is prepared to implement and maintain the latest technological advancements.
[...]
The curriculum of Tech Elevate includes a focus on artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, various programming languages, modern web development, and application programming interface (API) query languages. These areas are critical for building robust digital infrastructures that can support the company's ambitious growth plans.
[...]
Tech Elevate offers three specialized learning tracks to cater to the diverse needs of its employees. The first track, "AI-First Platform & Reliability Engineering," provides hands-on experience with agentic AI. This track allows employees to deploy services, troubleshoot failures, and build self-healing workflows, thereby integrating AI tools into their daily routines to enhance system resilience and scalability.
[...]
The final track focuses on the execution, federation, and best practices of API query languages. This course offers a comprehensive understanding of production-grade API platforms, enabling engineers to design, implement, and evolve APIs that are resilient, scalable, and aligned with organizational standards.
[...]
By focusing on upskilling its workforce, Yum! Brands is reinforcing its position as a leader in digital restaurant technology. The skills developed through Tech Elevate are pivotal in building top-tier technology solutions that can drive the company's digital transformation efforts. This strategic move ensures that Yum! is not only keeping pace with industry trends but is also setting new benchmarks for digital innovation in the QSR sector.
[...]
The program's emphasis on practical, immediately applicable skills has been well-received by participants. Saurabh Sundriyal, a senior software engineer at KFC U.S., noted that the AI-First Platform and Reliability Engineering course was particularly beneficial for his daily work, highlighting the program's direct impact on operational efficiency.
[...]
As Yum! Brands continues to invest in its workforce, the company is well-positioned to maintain its leadership in digital restaurant technology. Initiatives like Tech Elevate are crucial in equipping teams with the necessary skills to navigate the complexities of modern digital infrastructures. This focus on workforce development not only supports the company's growth objectives but also ensures that it remains at the forefront of technological innovation in the fast-food industry.
[...]
Saksham Gupta is the Co-Founder and Technology lead at Edubild. With extensive experience in enterprise AI, LLM systems, and B2B integration, he writes about the practical side of building AI products that work in production. Connect with him on LinkedIn for more insights on AI engineering and enterprise technology.

results.title: Yum Brands’ tech chief on building its ‘AI backbone’ | CIO Dive
highlights:
# Yum Brands’ tech chief on building its ‘AI backbone’
[...]
But much of the Taco Bell, KFC and Pizza Hut parent company’s digital strategy has been developed in just the last few years, its tech leader Jim Dausch said during Wednesday’s CIO and CX Dive virtual event on how AI is reshaping the enterprise. In 2019, only 1% of Taco Bell’s sales were digital, compared with nearly 50% in 2026, Dausch said.
[...]
Dausch transitioned roles in September to include president of Byte by Yum, the restaurant brand’s SaaS platform of AI-driven tools for customers, processes and internal teams. He also holds the role of Yum’s chief digital and technology officer, after having spent a year as global chief digital and technology officer of Pizza Hut.
[...]
Yum Brands spent much of 2025 rolling out AI tools to more of its restaurants, as it further built its AI strategy. It also partnered with tech giants, such as AWS, Microsoft and Nvidia, which it used to deploy voice AI agents, and built out its proprietary platform to become more AI focused.
[...]
AI is a “new and powerful technology” that can provide greater intelligence and real-time evolution and performance on top of the company’s existing tech stack, Dausch said. Because it had a decades-old technology foundation, the company began modernization efforts just before the COVID-19 pandemic for its more than 60,000 restaurants.
[...]
Byte by Yum was the company’s answer, an AI backbone behind the restaurants’ digital ordering platform, point of sale, kitchen delivery, back of house, inventory management and other core systems that Dausch said had been previously fragmented. It’s paired with the consumer-facing app that allows associates to provide personalized experiences at restaurants.
[...]
The Byte software
[...]
5,0
[...]
The company’s internal AI strategy is three-fold, according to Dausch. It’s aimed at driving higher speed of innovation and enterprise productivity, driving customer growth by embedding AI in traditional customer acquisition, conversion and retention lifecycle, and embedding AI within restaurants to simplify operations and to improve unit economics for franchisees.
[...]
Dausch said it’s easy to forget that AI is just one part of their overall business strategy and reminded other tech leaders not to lose sight of the real problems or outcomes a business is trying to solve.
[...]
“AI, at the end of the day, in my view, is still just a tool, as is the rest of technology,” Dausch said. “We focus on delivering great food at great value with great service to our customers. That's the business that we're in.”

results.title: Yum Brands’ tech chief on building its ‘AI backbone’ | Restaurant Dive
highlights:
# Yum Brands’ tech chief on building its ‘AI backbone’
[...]
The company underwent modernization efforts for its decades-old brands to use one common data model across 35,000 restaurants.
[...]
But much of the Taco Bell, KFC and Pizza Hut parent company’s digital strategy has been developed in just the last few years, its tech leader Jim Dausch said during a May CIO and CX Dive virtual event on how AI is reshaping the enterprise. In 2019, only 1% of Taco Bell’s sales were digital, compared with nearly 50% in 2026, Dausch said.
[...]
Dausch transitioned roles in September to include president of Byte by Yum, the restaurant brand’s SaaS platform of AI-driven tools for customers, processes and internal teams. He also holds the role of Yum’s chief digital and technology officer, after having spent a year as global chief digital and technology officer of Pizza Hut.
[...]
Yum Brands spent much of 2025 rolling out AI tools to more of its restaurants, as it further built its AI strategy. It also partnered with tech giants, such as AWS, Microsoft and Nvidia, which it used to deploy voice AI agents, and built out its proprietary platform to become more AI focused.
[...]
AI is a “new and powerful technology” that can provide greater intelligence and real-time evolution and performance on top of the company’s existing tech stack, Dausch said. Because it had a decades-old technology foundation, the company began modernization efforts just before the COVID-19 pandemic for its more than 60,000 restaurants.
[...]
Byte by Yum was the company’s answer, an AI backbone behind the restaurants’ digital ordering platform, point of sale, kitchen delivery, back of house, inventory management and other core systems that Dausch said had been previously fragmented. It’s paired with the consumer-facing app that allows associates to provide personalized experiences at restaurants.
[...]
Creating one common data model across all of the brand’s systems so data flows more seamlessly was essential, Dausch said, especially as some of the brands are more than 60 years old.
[...]
The Byte software is now used in more than 35,000 restaurants in 150 countries, and its digital ordering platform is in 18,000 restaurants and has processed about $20 billion in sales, Dausch said.
[...]
The company’s internal AI strategy is three-fold, according to Dausch. It’s aimed at driving higher speed of innovation and enterprise productivity, driving customer growth by embedding AI in traditional customer acquisition, conversion and retention lifecycle, and embedding AI within restaurants to simplify operations and to improve unit economics for franchisees.
[...]
Dausch said it’s easy to forget that AI is just one part of their overall business strategy and reminded other tech leaders not to lose sight of the real problems or outcomes a business is trying to solve.
[...]
“AI, at the end of the day, in my view, is still just a tool, as is the rest of technology,” Dausch said. “We focus on delivering great food at great value with great service to our customers. That's the business that we're in.”

results.title: Exclusive: Inside Yum's effort to predict what customers will crave next
highlights:
Exclusive: Inside Yum's effort to predict what customers will crave next

Oops, something went wrong

# Exclusive: Inside Yum's effort to predict what customers will crave next

Taco Bell's growing beverage business has become one area where Yum Brands uses data and prediction-market research to evaluate new concepts. Photo: Courtesy of Taco Bell/Yum Brands

Yum Brands is using AI, data science and predictive market research to identify potential menu winners across Taco Bell, KFC, Pizza Hut and Habit Burger & Grill, the company revealed exclusively with Axios.

Why it matters: Restaurants spend millions developing new menu items with no guarantee that customers will bite.

---

Yum's efforts come as restaurant chains increasingly embrace AI across their businesses, from McDonald's use of AI-powered personalization and Wendy's FreshAI drive-thru ordering technology.

Consumer tastes are also evolving faster than ever, with trends that once took years to spread now moving globally in a matter of weeks, according to Yum executives.

The big picture: At the center of the effort is Yum's Global Innovation Database, developed by Collider Lab, the company's in-house strategy and innovation group made up of social scientists, strategists and data scientists.

The database contains more than 7,000 food, beverage and marketing concepts evaluated across 35 countries.

The system uses predictive market research, asking consumers to forecast how others will respond to concepts rather than relying solely on traditional consumer research, Greg Dzurik, Yum's vice president of marketing and innovation strategy, told Axios.

Zoom in: The system has informed products including Pizza Hut Melts and KFC shakes, according to Yum. For KFC Saucy, the company tested both menu concepts and potential names before launch.

It has also been used to evaluate beverage concepts at Taco Bell, where drinks have become a key growth driver, according to Global Chief Food Innovation Officer Liz Matthews.

Taco Bell sold more than 950 million beverages in 2025, and the chain aims to build a $5 billion beverage business by 2030 as it expands beverage innovation through initiatives like Live Más Café, Matthews told Axios.

The database has surfaced broader consumer shifts, including demand for snackable foods, protein-focused products and specialty beverages.

What they're saying: Yum views the database as a competitive advantage because it allows teams to connect learnings across brands and markets, chief marketing officer Ken Muench said.

"Over time, every concept we test makes the system smarter, creating an innovation engine that's difficult to replicate," Muench said.

Muench said the company views AI as a tool that complements — rather than replaces — human creativity.

"Become too dependent on AI to make decisions and your marketing will eventually become more standard, less breakthrough, more expected," he said.

Story Continues

The bottom line: Yum executives say AI can help identify patterns and inspire ideas, but marketers, chefs and innovation teams still decide what ultimately makes it to menus.

"AI will give you the logic and humans will give you the magic," Dzurik said.

More from Axios:

Amazon is putting groceries at the center of Prime Day

T-Mobile turns T-Mobile Tuesdays into a broader loyalty platform

What Victoria's Secret and Dollar General have in common

AI is moving fast. Axios AI+ keeps you ahead. Sign up free at Axios.com.

View Comments

---

results.title: How Yum Brands Uses Predictive Analytics to Design Future Fast Food
highlights:
The quest to engineer the next viral fast-food sensation has moved out of the test kitchen and into the digital ether. Yum Brands is utilizing artificial intelligence, data science, and predictive market research to identify potential menu winners across its global portfolio, including Taco Bell, KFC, Pizza Hut, and Habit Burger & Grill.
[...]
Developing new menu items
[...]
The fast-food landscape is rapidly shifting as major restaurant chains increasingly embrace artificial intelligence across various facets of their operations. This technological wave includes McDonald's implementation of AI-powered personalization systems and Wendy's deployment of its FreshAI drive-thru ordering technology.
[...]
At the center of Yum Brands' technological initiative is its Global Innovation Database. The proprietary platform was developed by Collider Lab, an in-house strategy and innovation group composed of social scientists, corporate strategists, and data scientists.
[...]
The central database currently houses analytics for more than 7,000 food, beverage, and marketing concepts that have been evaluated across 35 different countries. Instead of relying solely on traditional consumer research methodologies, the system utilizes predictive market research to ask consumers to forecast how other people will respond to specific concepts, explained Greg Dzurik, Yum's vice president of marketing and innovation strategy.
[...]
The analytical platform has already shaped several high-profile product rollouts, including Pizza Hut Melts and KFC shakes. Prior to launching KFC Saucy, the system was used to rigorously test both the underlying menu concepts and the potential commercial names.
[...]
The analytical database has been heavily leveraged to evaluate liquid concepts at Taco Bell, where the beverage category has transformed into a critical growth engine for the brand. The system has surfaced deep consumer shifts toward snackable items, protein-focused products, and specialty liquid refreshments.
[...]
Taco Bell successfully sold more than 950 million beverages in 2025. The brand is now actively aiming to construct a $5 billion beverage enterprise by 2030 by expanding its liquid innovation pipeline through dedicated initiatives like Live Más Café, detailed global chief food innovation officer Liz Matthews.
[...]
Yum Brands views its centralized database as a substantial competitive advantage in the crowded fast-food marketplace. The platform allows distinct regional teams to instantly connect key behavioral learnings across different brands and geographical markets.

results.title: Yum Brands is putting Nvidia AI into 500 restaurants - Startup Fortune
highlights:
Yum Brands is putting Nvidia AI into 500 restaurants - Startup Fortune
[...]
# Yum Brands is putting Nvidia AI into 500 restaurants
[...]
Yum Brands is rolling out Nvidia-powered AI across 500 restaurants, turning a pilot into one of the clearest large-scale deployments in quick-service dining.
[...]
Yum Brands' Nvidia partnership is no longer just a 2025 rollout promise. The more useful story now is how the restaurant giant is turning AI into operating infrastructure across its brands.
[...]
Yum Brands has moved its AI work beyond the language of pilots and into the harder question of scale. The company behind Taco Bell, Pizza Hut, KFC and Habit Burger & Grill announced its Nvidia partnership in March 2025 with a goal of deploying AI systems across up to 500 restaurants that year. A year later, the sharper signal is that Yum is still talking about AI as a core operating capability, not a side project for a few test stores.
[...]
According to Nvidia and Yum's original announcement, the first wave focused on three practical uses: voice AI for drive-thru and call-center ordering, computer vision for restaurant operations, and AI-driven analytics for performance insights. Yum built voice ordering tools using Nvidia NIM microservices and Nvidia Riva, then connected that work to Byte by Yum!, its in-house digital and AI platform.
[...]
The important point is not that a fast-food chain is experimenting with voice ordering. That has been happening across the industry for years. The point is that Yum is trying to own more of the intelligence layer itself. That gives the company more control over restaurant data, workflow design and the pace at which new capabilities can be rolled out across brands.
[...]
Yum now operates more than 63,000 restaurants globally, which makes even a limited deployment meaningful. A system that works in a few hundred locations can teach the company more than a small lab test ever could, especially when those restaurants span different menus, customer habits and service models. Scale turns operational software into a learning system.
[...]
500
[...]
always small compared
[...]
global footprint, but it
[...]
As Yum recently noted in an update on Byte by Yum!, the company has been refining smaller language models for restaurant ordering, including work that improved accuracy in selecting the right tool or function during complex multi-step orders. That kind of detail is more useful than a broad AI claim because it shows where the hard work actually sits. The value is not in a chatbot sounding fluent. The value is in getting the order right when a customer changes a taco, adds a drink, swaps a side and expects the total to be correct.
[...]
For restaurant operators, this is where AI becomes practical. Better voice systems can reduce bottlenecks at peak times. Call-center automation can absorb demand spikes without forcing stores to choose between answering phones and serving customers in line. Computer vision can help managers see operational problems faster, from queue buildup to process breakdowns. None of that is glamorous, but it is exactly where margins are won or lost.
[...]
There is also a wider market message for Nvidia. The company is not only selling chips into the AI boom. With NIM microservices, Riva and Nvidia AI Enterprise, it is trying to become part of the software layer that large companies use to deploy AI in the real world. Yum gives Nvidia a consumer-facing proof point in a business where speed, accuracy and uptime are easy to measure.
[...]
That is why this story still matters in 2026. The headline is not simply that Yum put Nvidia technology into restaurants. It is that large service businesses are beginning to treat AI as part of their operating stack, in the same category as payments, scheduling and point-of-sale systems. Watch what Yum does next with Byte by Yum!, because the bigger test is whether AI can become boring, reliable infrastructure inside thousands of restaurants.

This methodology has already shaped multiple commercial releases, such as Pizza Hut Melts and KFC shakes. For the launch of KFC Saucy, the corporation utilized the platform to evaluate both the culinary concepts and the potential marketing names before the public rollout.

The database heavily influences beverage development at Taco Bell, where drinks serve as a primary vehicle for financial growth. Taco Bell recorded more than 950 million beverage sales in 2025, and the company intends to establish a $5 billion drink business by 2030 through specialized concepts like the Live Más Café.
[...]
Data tracking has highlighted broader shifts in consumer habits, specifically pointing toward increased demand for high-protein products, snackable items, and specialty drinks. Executives view the shared system as a distinct competitive benefit that links operational insights across multiple brands and geographic markets.

system smarter, creating an innovation engine that's difficult to replicate,"
[...]
ch said.

The corporate strategy treats artificial intelligence as a supplemental asset designed to support human creativity rather than replace it entirely.

results.title: Yum Brands uses AI and predictive research to develop menu items across its restaurant chains
highlights:
Yum Brands uses AI and predictive research to develop menu items across its restaurant chains
[...]
# Yum Brands uses AI and predictive research to develop menu items across its restaurant chains
[...]
Yum Brands uses an AI-powered database of 7,000+ tested concepts to predict which menu items will sell across Taco Bell, KFC, and Pizza Hut. The system asks consumers to forecast others' reactions, rather than just their own preferences.
[...]
## Yum Brands Builds AI System to Predict Menu Winners Across Restaurant Chains
[...]
Yum Brands uses artificial intelligence and predictive market research to identify which menu items will succeed across Taco Bell, KFC, Pizza Hut, and Habit Burger. The company disclosed the practice to Axios in June 2026.
[...]
The approach addresses a persistent problem
[...]
new menu development
[...]
of dollars with no
[...]
production and marketing.
[...]
Yum's Global Innovation Database, built by Collider Lab (the company's internal strategy and innovation group), contains over 7,000 food, beverage, and marketing concepts tested across 35 countries. The database uses data analysis and predictive market research methods that ask consumers to forecast how others will respond to concepts, rather than relying solely on traditional consumer surveys.
[...]
Greg Dzurik, Yum's vice president of marketing and innovation strategy, said the predictive approach differs from asking consumers directly what they want. The system identifies patterns across markets and brands that individual market research might miss.
[...]
Yum's approach fits a wider pattern of restaurant chains integrating AI into operations. McDonald's uses AI for personalization, while Wendy's deployed FreshAI technology for drive-thru ordering.
[...]
The database has identified consumer shifts toward snackable foods, protein-focused products, and specialty beverages. Ken Muench, Yum's chief marketing officer, said the system creates a competitive advantage by allowing teams to share learnings across brands and markets.
[...]
### AI as Tool, Not Replacement
[...]
Muench cautioned against overreliance on AI for decision-making. He said the company views AI as a tool that complements human creativity rather than replacing it, noting that excessive dependence on automated systems can produce marketing that becomes standard, predictable, and less distinctive.
[...]
Each concept tested in the database makes the system smarter over time, creating what Muench described as an innovation engine that competitors find difficult to replicate.
[...]
Yum executives noted that consumer tastes now shift globally within weeks-a pace that previously took years. The database helps the company track and respond to those changes across its portfolio.

results.title: Yum Brands And Nvidia Launch Restaurant AI Partnership | Ubergizmo
highlights:
Yum Brands And Nvidia Launch Restaurant AI Partnership | Ubergizmo

# Yum Brands And Nvidia Launch Restaurant AI Partnership

Yum Brands has partnered with tech company Nvidia to accelerate the development and deployment of artificial intelligence across its restaurant network. As the technology company’s first restaurant partner, Yum Brands will use Nvidia’s software to enhance its proprietary technology system, known as Byte. Following early pilots at selected Pizza Hut and Taco Bell locations, the company plans a broader rollout to approximately 500 stores—including KFC and Habit Burger units—during the second quarter.

The collaboration focuses primarily on three operational areas: voice AI for drive-thrus and call centers, computer vision for operational analysis, and restaurant-level data analytics. By utilizing specialized microservices, the voice ordering system is designed to navigate complex menus and varied speech patterns. Meanwhile, the computer vision component will analyze back-of-house labor management and drive-thru efficiency, generating real-time analytics and alerts. Yum Brands intends for these combined technologies to eventually evolve into autonomous AI agents capable of planning, reasoning, and assisting with daily restaurant tasks.

Additionally, the partnership introduces “Accelerated Restaurant Intelligence,” an analytics tool that compiles data from high-performing stores to generate actionable improvement plans for managers at struggling locations. By leveraging Nvidia’s infrastructure, Yum Brands aims to lower costs and increase the deployment speed of digital tools for its franchisees. The systems will utilize the extensive transaction data generated across Yum’s global network of 61,000 stores, and all tools developed through the partnership will remain proprietary to the company.

This initiative aligns with Yum Brands’ long-term strategy of using corporate acquisitions and internal development to maintain a technological edge in the quick-service restaurant industry. The company has already introduced voice AI at hundreds of Taco Bell drive-thrus and expanded its digital kitchen and delivery management systems to thousands of global locations. Ultimately, Yum Brands aims to integrate proprietary digital technology into every operational touchpoint across its global brands.

Filed in

. Read more about AI (Artificial Intelligence) and NVIDIA.

Our mission is to help large successful brands like Uber, Amazon, Wise, HelloFresh (and more!) put their customers at the centre of everything they do. We’re hiring a Senior Data Scientist to join the team and help build and ship the next generation of that stack.

Unlike many companies, we use our own custom models, specialised for customer feedback, across various parts of the stack: extraction, retrieval, reranking, summarisation, and sentiment analysis. [Hiring] Data Scientist REMOTE USA - Gulf Career Hunt

24 May 2026

Position: Data Scientist

Date Posted: May 24, 2026

Industry: Data Science / Market Research / Analytics / FinTech

Employment Type: Full Time

Experience: Not Specified

Qualification: Not Specified

Location: United States (Remote)

Company: YipitData

Description:

YipitData is a leading market research and analytics firm specializing in the disruptive economy. The company transforms massive alternative data sets into actionable insights used by top investment funds and Fortune 500 organizations to support high-impact financial and strategic decisions.

The company is currently hiring a Data Scientist to work on advanced analytical projects involving causal inference, predictive modeling, and large-scale alternative data processing. This role is highly technical and combines traditional data science with AI-native workflows, including the use of LLM-based tools to accelerate research, coding, and analysis.

The ideal candidate will work end-to-end on complex data science problems, translating ambiguous business questions into structured analytical solutions while producing high-quality insights, models, and written research outputs for institutional clients.

Key Responsibilities:

• Translate complex business questions into structured data science problems.

• Build and analyze predictive and causal models using large-scale datasets.

• Work with alternative data sources including transaction, invoice, and web-scraped data.

• Develop feature engineering pipelines using tools like Spark.

• Produce technical white papers and analytical reports for clients.

• Use LLM tools to enhance coding, analysis, and workflow efficiency.

• Build internal AI-driven tools and evaluation systems.

• Collaborate with engineering, product, and business teams on data solutions.

• Establish and maintain high technical standards for data science workflows.

Requirements:

• Proven experience delivering end-to-end data science projects with measurable impact.

• Strong understanding of causal inference and statistical modeling techniques.

• Advanced Python programming skills with clean, production-quality code.

• Experience working with large datasets and distributed computing tools.

• Strong writing and communication skills for technical documentation.

• Familiarity with LLM tools and AI-assisted development workflows.

• Strong knowledge of:

• Causal Inference and Statistical Modeling

• Predictive Analytics and Machine Learning

• Python, Spark, and Data Engineering Tools

Description & Requirements Who we are lululemon is an innovative performance apparel company for yoga, running, training, and other athletic pursuits. Setting the bar in technical fabrics and functional design, we create transformational products and experiences that support people in moving, growing, connecting, and being well. We owe our success to our innovative product, emphasis on stores, commitment to our people, and the incredible connections we make in every community we're in. About this team The Enterprise Data & AI team is a strategic and operational driver of growth for lululemon, owning and building the data and AI platforms and products that enable the enterprise to operate with intelligence at scale. The team leads the design and delivery of a trusted unified data foundation, advanced analytics capabilities, and AI solutions across lululemon's vertically integrated retail ecosystem, embedding strong data governance and responsible AI practices from the very beginning. By applying AI to critical business challenges and creating new, transformative AI solutions, the team helps reshape how lululemon operates. Through deep partnership with product, technology, and business teams, Enterprise Data & AI accelerates product innovation, unlocks measurable value, elevates guest and educator experiences, and drives enterprise efficiency. highlights:
So far, I've scraped and analyzed 3K+ Data Scientist job postings from recent months to understand how AI is shaping the data science job market as we continue 2026 Here are some key takeaways: 1. 66% of all postings now expect some level of AI capability. 2. Experience with LLMs was the number 1 AI skill in demand. 3. 4 in 10 AI-related roles require hands-on experience with multiple areas: LLMs, RAG, prompt engineering, vector databases, etc. 4. 73% of postings mentioning AI target mid or senior-level roles. 5. AI expectations are baked into standard Data Scientist roles, not just new titles. 6. Median salaries for AI-skilled Data Scientists are up 5–12% compared to non-AI-focused data scientist roles (This is still preliminary) I've been following the AI + Data Scientist job market closely since early last year, but this is the first time I've done a deep analysis like this One thing is very clear: AI is becoming a core part of the data scientist role, and it is already influencing expectations, scope, and career progression. 📌 Btw, if you are a data scientist trying to future-proof your career, get my free workshop on "Agentic Analytics in Production": https://lnkd.in/d3yBufrY
